---
title: "How a marine biologist learned to write for everyone"
page-layout: article
toc: false
---

<div class="sc-page-hero">
  <p class="sc-eyebrow">Episode 41 · Apr 21, 2025</p>
  <h1>How a marine biologist learned to write for everyone</h1>
  <div style="display:flex;gap:14px;margin-top:16px;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:13px;color:var(--ink-faint);">
    <span>⏱ 52 min</span><span>🎙 Diana Chen, Maya Patel</span>
  </div>
  <div style="display:flex;gap:10px;margin-top:20px;">
    <a href="#" class="btn btn-dark">🎙 Listen on Spotify</a>
    <a href="#" class="btn btn-outline">Apple Podcasts</a>
  </div>
</div>

<div style="max-width:700px;margin:0 auto;padding:0 40px 60px;">

Dr. Diana Chen studies coral resilience in warming oceans. Her papers are impeccably written — for an audience of roughly 400 other specialists worldwide.

When she tried to explain her work to her parents at a family dinner in 2019 and watched their eyes glaze over, she realised that the most important science she was doing might never reach the people it mattered to most.

This is the story of how she learned to write differently — and what she lost and gained in the process.

## Topics covered

- The moment Diana realised academic writing wasn't enough
- How to translate without dumbing down  
- Finding your voice outside peer review
- Why vulnerability in science writing builds, not undermines, credibility

</div>

<section class="sc-about-strip">
  <h2>More episodes</h2>
  <a href="index.qmd" class="btn btn-outline">All episodes →</a>
</section>
